report_name: Atomic Stealer Malware Bypasses macOS Warnings with New 'ClickFix' Attack Vector

attack_patterns.name: User Execution
attack_patterns.description: Victims are tricked through a social engineering technique called 'ClickFix' into pasting and running malicious code within Apple's Script Editor, believing it to be a legitimate troubleshooting step.

attack_patterns.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: AppleScript
attack_patterns.description: Attackers instruct users to paste and execute a malicious AppleScript via the Script Editor. The script likely uses a 'do shell script' command to download and execute the Atomic Stealer payload.

attack_patterns.name: Impair Defenses
attack_patterns.description: Attackers pivoted from using the Terminal application to using Apple's Script Editor to execute code, successfully bypassing a new security warning Apple introduced in macOS 26.4 for pasting commands into the Terminal.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Password Stores
attack_patterns.description: The Atomic Stealer malware is capable of harvesting passwords from all major browsers on a compromised macOS system.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
attack_patterns.description: Atomic Stealer steals browser cookies and session tokens, allowing attackers to hijack active sessions for various online accounts.

attack_patterns.name: Launch Agent
attack_patterns.description: The malware installs a backdoor for persistent access, likely by creating a file in a directory such as ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ to ensure it runs automatically.

malware.name: Atomic Stealer
malware.description: A potent infostealer targeting macOS, delivered via a social engineering campaign named 'ClickFix'. It harvests browser passwords, cookies, crypto wallets, and system information, and installs a persistent backdoor.

tools.name: Apple Script Editor
tools.description: Abused as part of the 'ClickFix' social engineering attack. Victims are tricked into pasting and executing malicious code in this trusted, built-in macOS application to bypass Terminal security warnings.

tools.name: osascript
tools.description: The command-line utility for executing AppleScripts. This process is spawned when a user runs a script from the Script Editor, and can be monitored to detect malicious activity.

identities.name: Apple
identities.description: Vendor of the targeted macOS operating system and the abused Script Editor application. The company had previously added security warnings to the Terminal application, which the attackers bypassed.

infrastructure.name: Atomic Stealer C2 Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Command and control servers used by the Atomic Stealer malware to exfiltrate stolen data and receive commands.

indicators.name: Suspicious AppleScript Execution via osascript
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious AppleScript Execution Downloading Remote File
id: 9b1d8c3a-7e4f-4b1a-9c8d-6e5f4a3b2c1d
status: experimental
description: Detects 'osascript' executing a shell script that uses 'curl' to download a file, a TTP associated with the Atomic Stealer 'ClickFix' campaign.
references:
  - https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/atomic-stealer-macos-clickfix/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-09
tags:
  - attack.t1059.002
  - attack.t1105
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: macos
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith: '/osascript'
    Image|endswith: '/curl'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate scripts that use osascript to download files.
indicators.description: Detects the execution of 'osascript' which then spawns a 'curl' process to download a remote payload, as seen in the Atomic Stealer campaign.

indicators.name: macOS Persistence via LaunchAgents
indicators.pattern:
title: macOS Persistence via LaunchAgents Creation
id: a2c3b4d5-e6f7-4890-a1b2-c3d4e5f6a7b8
status: experimental
description: Detects the creation of files in the ~/Library/LaunchAgents directory, a common persistence technique used by macOS malware like Atomic Stealer.
references:
  - https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/atomic-stealer-macos-clickfix/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-09
tags:
  - attack.t1543.001
level: medium
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: macos
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|startswith: '~/Library/LaunchAgents/'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate application installers creating LaunchAgents for legitimate purposes.
indicators.description: Detects file creation in the `~/Library/LaunchAgents/` directory, a common persistence mechanism for macOS malware.

indicators.name: osascript Process Execution
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: osascript Execution Detected
id: f1e2d3c4-b5a6-4987-8654-3210fedcba98
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of osascript, the AppleScript command-line executor. While often legitimate, it was abused in the Atomic Stealer campaign.
references:
  - https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/atomic-stealer-macos-clickfix/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-09
tags:
  - attack.t1059.002
level: informational
logsource:
  product: macos
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '/osascript'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate use by administrators or applications.
